title: Demand
description: Questions on demand, the law of demand, and the determinants of demand

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following will result in a shift in a demand curve except:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A change in the income of buyers
A change in the supply of a good
A change in expected future prices
A change in the price of related goods
A change in buyers' preferences

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the two following movements in this graph would represent an increase in quantity demanded but not a change in demand?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two downward-sloping straight lines labeled D 1 and D 2. The horizontal axis is labeled Q, and the vertical axis is labeled P, both ranging from 0 to 12 with grid lines marked at every 1 unit. Line D 1 is between (0, 9) and (9, 0) and passes through the points S (2, 7) and U (6, 3). Line D 2 is between (0, 12) and (12, 0) and passes through the points R (5, 7) and T (9, 3). Horizontal dashed lines extend from (0, 7) to point T through point S and from (0, 3) to point T through point U. Vertical dotted lines extend from point S to the Q-axis at (2, 0), from point R to (5, 0), from point U to (6, 0), and from point T to the Q-axis at (9, 0).
options.caption: The demand for Good $X$

choices.content: From point $S$ to point $R$
From point $U$ to point $T$
From point $R$ to point $U$
From point $S$ to point $U$
From point $S$ to point $T$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following movements would represent an increase in the demand for a good?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two downward-sloping straight lines labeled D 1 and D 2. The horizontal axis is labeled Q, and the vertical axis is labeled P, both ranging from 0 to 12 with grid lines marked at every 1 unit. Line D 1 is between (0, 9) and (9, 0) and passes through the points S (2, 7) and U (6, 3). Line D 2 is between (0, 12) and (12, 0) and passes through the points R (5, 7) and T (9, 3). Horizontal dashed lines extend from (0, 7) to point T through point S and from (0, 3) to point T through point U. Vertical dotted lines extend from point S to the Q-axis at (2, 0), from point R to (5, 0), from point U to (6, 0), and from point T to the Q-axis at (9, 0).

choices.content: From $S$ to $R$
From $S$ to $U$
From $T$ to $U$
From $R$ to $T$
From $R$ to $S$

assessment_items.question_content:
*Something has changed about how consumers buy hats which resulted in the change shown in this graph.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following changes could this graph represent?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid with grid lines at each unit plots two downward-sloping lines labeled D 1 and D 2. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and ranges from 0 to 5 in increments of 1. The vertical axis is labeled P and ranges from 0 to 9 in increments of 1. Line D 2 starts at (0, 6) and ends at (2, 0). Line D 1 starts at (0, 9) and ends at (3, 0). A left-pointing arrow is placed between the two lines, indicating a shift from D 1 to D 2.
options.caption: $\text{Figure }1$: The demand for hats

choices.content: People do not like hats as much as they did before, so their demand has decreased
People do not like hats as much as they did before, resulting in a decrease in the quantity demanded for hats
People like hats more than they did before, and their demand for hats has increased
There has been a decrease in the price of hats, resulting in an increase in the quantity demanded for hats
People do not like hats as much as they did before, resulting in an increase in the quantity demanded for hats

assessment_items.question_content:
Penny thinks that the price of books will decrease next week. 

**If everything else is equal, which of the following would reflect how Penny's behavior will change in response to her expectations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Penny will move to a new, higher quantity on her demand curve
Penny will move to a new, lower quantity along her demand curve
Penny's demand for books today will increase
Penny's demand for books today will decrease
Penny will demand more complementary goods to books

assessment_items.question_content:
The demand for Good $X$ is given in the graph shown here.

**Which of the following could cause the shift of demand shown here?**

[[☃ image 1]]

 [[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid with grid lines at each unit plots two downward-sloping lines labeled D 1 and D 2. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and ranges from 0 to 12 in increments of 1. The vertical axis is labeled P and ranges from 0 to 12 in increments of 1. Line D 1 begins at (0, 9) and ends at (9, 0). Line D 2 begins at (0, 12) and ends at (12, 0). A right-pointing arrow between the lines indicates a shift from D 1 to D 2.
options.caption: The demand for Good $X$

choices.content: An increase in the price of good $Y$, which is a complement to good $X$
An increase in income if good $X$ is an inferior good
An increase in the number of buyers of a good
Buyers of good $X$ expect the price of good $X$ to decrease in the future
People do not like good $X$ as much as they did before

assessment_items.question_content:
Economists have observed that when average incomes increase, purchases of fast food tends to decline. 

**Based on this information, what can we definitely say about fast food?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Demand for fast food is upward sloping
Fast food has no substitutes
The price of a complement to fast food has risen
Fast food is an inferior good
Fast food is a normal good

assessment_items.question_content:
Meeps and Blops are two goods that are related to each other.  When the price of Meeps goes down, the demand for Blops goes down.

**Based only on the information given here, what kind of goods are Meeps and Blops?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: complements
substitutes
inferior goods
normal goods
superior goods

assessment_items.question_content:
As a result of an increase in consumer incomes, the demand for Meeps has decreased. 

**Based on this information, what can we definitely say about what type of good a Meep is?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It is a normal good.
It is an inferior good.
It is a substitute good.
It is a complement good.
It has many substitutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pam considers t-shirts and gym classes to be complementary goods. 

**How will Pam react if the price of gym classes increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Her quantity demanded of gym classes will decrease, and her demand for t-shirts will decrease.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will increase, and her demand for t-shirts will decrease.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will decrease, and her demand for t-shirts will be unaffected.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will increase, and her demand for t-shirts will increase.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes won’t change, and her demand for t-shirts will increase.
